task_context.description: This is a particle detector design and performance evaluation task focused on creating a muon spectrometer capable of distinguishing between muons and pions. The task involves designing detector configurations with alternating absorber and tracking layers, then conducting comparative simulations to measure detection efficiency, particle stopping power, and energy deposition patterns. The user needs to test multiple detector geometries and absorber materials to determine the optimal configuration for muon identification while rejecting pion backgrounds. This is both a design exploration and a physics performance characterization study.
task_context.goal: Design and evaluate a muon spectrometer that can effectively identify muons while rejecting pions, determining the muon/pion separation power through comparative measurements
constraints: Energy sweep capped at 50 GeV maximum
Limited to 3 energy points for simulation tractability
Use parallel GPU simulation for efficiency
Absorber thickness should be 1-3 interaction lengths (not 8-12 like hadronic calorimeters)

observations.content: QUALITY GATE FAILED: deterministic physics violations (1). Extracted metrics: {'muon_detection_efficiency_iron_5GeV': '100%', 'muon_detection_efficiency_iron_20GeV': '100%', 'muon_detection_efficiency_iron_50GeV': '100%', 'pion_stopping_probability_iron_5GeV': '98.35%', 'pion_stopping_probability_iron_20GeV': '97.97%', 'pion_stopping_probability_iron_50GeV': '96.68%', 'muon_hits_iron_5GeV': '867.1', 'muon_hits_iron_20GeV': '1289.6', 'muon_hits_iron_50GeV': '1708.7', 'pion_hits_iron_5GeV': '8622.2', 'pion_hits_iron_20GeV': '35865.8', 'pion_hits_iron_50GeV': '89647.6', 'energy_resolution_iron_5GeV': '0.134', 'energy_resolution_iron_20GeV': '0.426', 'energy_resolution_iron_50GeV': '0.866', 'separation_power_iron_5GeV': '4.90', 'separation_power_iron_20GeV': '6.40', 'separation_power_iron_50GeV': '5.72', 'muon_detection_efficiency_aluminum_5GeV': '100%', 'muon_detection_efficiency_aluminum_20GeV': '100%', 'muon_detection_efficiency_aluminum_50GeV': '100%', 'pion_stopping_probability_aluminum_5GeV': '87.14%', 'pion_stopping_probability_aluminum_20GeV': '87.08%', 'pion_stopping_probability_aluminum_50GeV': '86.70%', 'separation_power_aluminum_5GeV': '1.97', 'separation_power_aluminum_20GeV': '1.68', 'separation_power_aluminum_50GeV': '1.55', 'total_events_per_energy': '10000', 'energy_points_tested': '3 (5, 20, 50 GeV)', 'detector_configurations': '3 (iron_box, aluminum_box, steel_tracking)', 'optimal_configuration': 'iron_box', 'iron_box_performance_score': '72.37'}. Verifier reasoning: All deliverables exist and results are physically meaningful. Three detector configurations were designed and tested: iron_box, aluminum_box, and steel_tracking. Both muons and pions were simulated at 5, 20, and 50 GeV with 10,000 events per energy point (adequate statistics). Hit counts are appropriate for muon spectrometers: muons produce 867-1709 hits while pions produce 5074-89648 hits, showing clear discrimination. Muon detection efficiency is 100% across all configurations and energies. Pion stopping probabilities are high (86.7-98.4%), demonstrating effective pion rejection. Energy resolution values (0.134-0.866) are reasonable for sampling calorimeters. The iron_box configuration achieved the highest separation power (5.67 average) and was correctly identified as optimal. All physics quantities are within expected ranges and no quality standards are violated.
